groups.title: Getting Started
groups.description: Learn the basics of cooperative exchange

slides.title: Before anyone named it
slides.body:
Two people. One helps the other. Something exists afterward that didn’t exist before.

This has always happened. Before money. Before barter. Before any system anyone ever invented. Two people, and the honest moment between them.

A neighbor watches your kids. A friend drives you to the airport. Someone shares a meal. Energy moved. That’s a cooperative act. It always was.

slides.title: The permission system
slides.body:
The monetary system didn’t just create a medium of exchange. It created a permission system for what counts as work.

Caregiving. Domestic labor. Community maintenance. Creative practice. All reclassified as leisure or charity — not because they produce no value, but because they produce no extractable value for the system doing the classifying.

HEP refuses this permission system entirely.

slides.title: The tarp
slides.body:
Two homeless people. One has a tarp. One needs it. Energy moves.

That is a cooperative act. It always was. The monetary system didn’t invent it — it just stopped counting it.

Nobody loses when that exchange gets recorded. No pool is drained. No authority is threatened. Two people’s reality becomes slightly more legible. That is all. And that is everything.

slides.title: The honest record
slides.body:
HEP records cooperative acts between people. What you did, with whom, when — confirmed by both parties, held only by the people who lived it.

Not a currency. Not a score. A record.

Before money existed, communities remembered who contributed and who received. This is that memory — made portable, tamper-evident, and held by the people who made it.

slides.title: You declare
slides.body:
When you provide something — mow a lawn, fix a pipe, teach a lesson, share a skill — you name the value. A number that represents the energy you spent.

No institution tells you what your work is worth. No algorithm decides. You declare it, and the other person either agrees or you have a conversation.

This is how valuation worked before anyone decided for everyone.

slides.title: Both sides honest
slides.body:
Every act has a provider and a receiver. Both are honest positions.

A teacher provides for years before receiving. A student receives for years before giving. A person in crisis receives care. None of this is a burden — it’s honest receiving.

The words are simple: provided and received. No moral weight. Just honest direction.

slides.title: Your chain is your reference
slides.body:
Over time your chain builds a record of what you’ve valued and what others have valued when working with you. Your own history becomes your pricing guide.

Fixed three faucets last month at 15 each? That’s your context for the next one. The pattern is yours. Nobody else sets it.

Price discovery emerges naturally from honest history. No authority required.

slides.title: No wrong number
slides.body:
A haircut might be worth 10 in one context and 25 in another. The protocol records what you agreed on, not what someone decided for you.

There is no correct price for anything. There is only what two people honestly agreed happened between them. The chain doesn’t judge. It records the cooperative reality.

That’s the point. The value is yours.

slides.title: The timing problem
slides.body:
You fix your neighbor’s sink. She’s grateful, but she can’t help you right now — and what you actually need is someone to tutor your kid.

This is the oldest problem in cooperation. Economists call it the coincidence of wants: both people need to want what the other has, at the same time, in the right proportion.

For most of human history, communities solved this with memory. Everyone knew who contributed and who received. That memory was the ledger.

slides.title: What money solved
slides.body:
Community memory worked, but it had limits. It couldn’t scale beyond the people who knew you.

Money solved this by creating a universal token — help someone, receive a coin, spend that coin with a stranger across town. You no longer needed the same person to help you back. The timing problem was solved.

But money solved it by creating a permission system. If the system doesn’t recognize your work, you don’t get the token. No token, no participation. The solution created its own exclusions.

slides.title: What your chain solves
slides.body:
Your chain is a portable record of your cooperative history. When someone who’s never met you reads it, they can see the shape of who you are — what you’ve done, how consistently, in what categories.

You fix the sink. Your chain records it. Next week you meet a tutor. She doesn’t know your neighbor. She doesn’t need to. She reads your chain and sees a real person with a real history of honest cooperation. A thread forms between you.

The timing problem is solved. No universal token required.

slides.title: No one is excluded
slides.body:
Here’s the difference: you don’t need a balance or a surplus to participate. Your chain doesn’t check your account before letting you cooperate.

A person who has received far more than they’ve provided — a student, an elder, someone recovering from crisis — still has a readable chain. Still has a history. Still can cooperate with anyone willing.

Money solved the coincidence of wants but created gatekeeping. The fabric of cooperation forms without the gate.

slides.title: Your starting point
slides.body:
Before your first exchange, it helps to think about what your unit means to you.

This is a thought exercise. There are no right answers. You’re building your own internal compass — a sense of relative value that’s yours alone.

Think of the simplest helpful thing you do regularly. Something easy. Something you wouldn’t think twice about.

Describe it in a few words:
Give it a number:
Pick any number that feels right. This becomes your anchor.

slides.title: Your skilled work
slides.body:
Now think of the most skilled thing you do. Something that took years to learn, or that very few people around you can do.

Describe it:

How many of your simple acts does this equal? If your simple act is your number, what’s this worth?

Its value:

slides.title: Something in between
slides.body:
Most of what you do probably falls somewhere between those two. Think of an everyday task that takes real effort but isn’t your most specialized work.

Describe it:
Its value:

slides.title: Something you’d receive
slides.body:
You won’t just be providing — you’ll be receiving too. Think of something you’d want from someone else.

What would you receive?
What feels fair?
There’s no wrong answer. This is about your sense of proportion.

slides.title: Your map
slides.body:
Here’s the internal map you just built — your own sense of relative value.

This isn’t a price list. It’s a compass. When you do your first real exchange and someone asks “what’s this worth?” — you already have an answer that’s yours.

Your chain will grow from here. Every real exchange refines your sense of value. This was just the starting point.

slides.title: How does an exchange work?
slides.body:
One person provides a service, a product, or help. The other person receives it. Both agree on a value — a number that represents what just happened.

You declare the value of your own work. Nobody tells you what it’s worth. The other person agrees or you negotiate.

The act gets recorded on both chains. That’s it. No money changed hands. Real work got recorded.

slides.title: Chain, thread, fabric
slides.body:
Your chain is your complete history of cooperative acts. Every exchange you’ve ever done, in order, linked together so nothing can be changed after the fact. Your balance shows whether you’ve provided more than you’ve received, or the reverse. Both are honest positions.

Every time you cooperate with someone, a thread is woven between your chain and theirs. The act sits on both, linking you. You might never see that person again, or you might exchange with them a hundred times. Each act is another thread.

When threads multiply across a community, they form a fabric — the cooperation that was always there, now visible. Nobody owns the fabric. It emerges from the acts.

A teacher gives for years before receiving. A student receives for years before giving. The chain holds the full arc. The threads show who cooperated. The fabric is what a community is, made legible.

slides.title: Two phones, one exchange
slides.body:
An exchange can happen several ways. If you are together, you exchange short pairing codes and your phones connect through the network. Both phones record the act. Both chains grow by one entry.

If there is no internet, you can use QR codes directly between phones. The record lives on your phone and theirs. Nobody else has a copy unless you choose to share it.

slides.title: What can you imagine?
slides.body:
A neighborhood where every act of cooperation is visible. A community that can see what it’s actually capable of. An elder whose lifetime of giving is finally recorded.

Your imagination is the limit. This tool records cooperative acts — what you build with it is up to you and your community.

Start by doing what you’re already doing. Just record it this time.

slides.title: Your chain is your reference
slides.body:
Over time, your chain builds a record of what you’ve provided and what you’ve received. When negotiating a new exchange, you can surface comparable acts from your own history.

Fixed three faucets last month at 15 each? That’s your pricing context. The other person can see the pattern without seeing who those acts were with.

Price discovery emerges naturally from honest history.

slides.title: Negotiation is human
slides.body:
Two people might value the same work differently. That’s not a flaw — it’s how real exchange works. The protocol records what you agreed on, not what someone decided for you.

A haircut might be worth 10 in one context and 25 in another. The chain doesn’t judge. It records the cooperative reality between two specific people at a specific moment.

slides.title: The chain viewer
slides.body:
When someone shares their chain with you before an exchange, you can see the shape of their work — what categories they work in, how consistently they price, how active they’ve been.

This is the context that makes negotiation informed rather than blind. You’re not guessing. You’re reading an honest record.

slides.title: The exchange rate
slides.body:
Every person values their effort on their own scale. Your “10” and someone else’s “10” might represent very different amounts of energy. The exchange rate translates between your scale and theirs.

Think of the way currencies work between countries. A dollar and a euro measure differently. Neither is wrong. The exchange rate makes sure both sides are represented honestly.

Your rate comes from your chain’s density compared to theirs. As both chains grow, the rate gets more precise.

slides.title: How parity works
slides.body:
When two people exchange, the rate between their chains adjusts prices so both sides are honestly represented. This happens automatically from the data. Nobody sets the rate.

Over time, your chain accumulates valuation data from everyone you have exchanged with. That accumulated data forms a basket, similar to what economists use to calculate purchasing power parity between countries, except nobody built or governs it.

Within specific categories of work, the rates are even more precise.

slides.title: Trust across chains
slides.body:
You don’t need to know someone to exchange with them. You need to see their chain. A long, active, balanced chain is hard to fake and easy to trust.

The protocol doesn’t vouch for anyone. The chain speaks for itself. A person with 500 confirmed acts over two years tells a different story than a person with 3 acts last week.

Trust is built honestly through cooperation. Fabrication is expensive to sustain.

slides.title: Planting the first seeds
slides.body:
Every network starts with two people doing one honest exchange. You don’t need critical mass. You need one real act.

Start with what’s already happening — a neighbor who watches your kids, a friend who fixes your car, a colleague who covers your shift. Record what was already real.

The protocol doesn’t create cooperation. It makes existing cooperation visible.

slides.title: The network grows naturally
slides.body:
When your counterparties start recording their own acts with others, the network spreads without anyone managing it. There is no sign-up, no onboarding funnel, no growth team.

Each person’s chain is independent. Threads form when people exchange. As more people cooperate, the threads weave into a fabric — the community, made visible. The community emerges from the acts themselves, not from a platform.

slides.title: What communities can see
slides.body:
When enough people in a neighborhood are recording cooperative acts, the community can see what it’s actually capable of.

How much tutoring happens every week. How many meals get shared. How much repair work circulates. The invisible economy becomes visible — not to surveil, but to understand and strengthen.

A community that can see its own cooperation can organize around it.

slides.title: Offline by design
slides.body:
This app works without the internet. Your chain lives on your device. Exchanges happen face-to-face. No server, no cloud, no dependency.

This isn’t a limitation — it’s the design. A system that requires infrastructure excludes everyone without access to it. A system that runs on any phone includes everyone.

slides.title: Your data stays yours
slides.body:
No one can access your chain without your PIN. No company stores a copy. No government has a backdoor. If you delete it, it’s gone.

This is data sovereignty in its simplest form. The person who created the data controls the data. There is no terms of service. There is no privacy policy to read. There is nothing to agree to because no one else is involved.

slides.title: Resilience through simplicity
slides.body:
The entire application is a single file. It runs on any phone with a browser. There is no server to hack, no database to breach, no company to shut down.

If the website disappears tomorrow, every installed copy keeps working. The protocol lives wherever the people are. That’s the point.

slides.title: You control what others see
slides.body:
When you share your chain for negotiation, no counterparty names are ever shown. Only your work patterns, categories, and pricing history.

The person you’re negotiating with can see the shape of your work without knowing who you worked with. Your competence travels with you. Your connections stay private.

slides.title: Protection in dangerous places
slides.body:
If you’re in a situation where revealing your network could be dangerous, the protocol protects you by design.

No central registry knows you exist. No list of members can be seized. Your thread is encrypted behind your PIN. To an outside observer, the app is just a file on your phone.

If you need to disappear, delete your thread. It’s gone. No trace, no record, no recovery.

slides.title: Identity without identification
slides.body:
Your fingerprint — a short hash of your public key — identifies you in exchanges. It proves you’re the same person across acts without revealing who you are.

You can participate fully without ever giving your real name. The declarations section is optional. What you share is what you choose to share. Nothing more.
